sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports a strong desire to quit smoking, citing both health concerns (gout, type 2 diabetes) and the impending arrival of his daughter. He previously attempted cold turkey, successfully quitting for nearly a year before experiencing stress-related relapse due to workplace layoffs. He's currently smoking approximately a pack and a half per day and has been smoking for some time now, starting in high school. He's learning meditation and recently joined a gym to manage stress. He's considering using a nicotine patch for cessation. He states he's considering names for his daughter and is waiting to see which name fits.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Blood pressure is 128/88 mmHg. Heart rate is 68 bpm. Pulse oximetry reading is 98% on room air. Oxygen saturation is 98%. Lung exam reveals clear and equal bilateral lung sounds with no wheezes or rales. Heart exam reveals a two over six systolic murmur. No lymphadenopathy is palpated.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The primary problem is nicotine dependence, exacerbated by stress. The patient's history of gout and type 2 diabetes contributes to his overall health risk. He is motivated to quit and is actively seeking strategies for stress management.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: I will prescribe a 21 mg nicotine patch to aid in quitting. The patient should change the patch location daily to avoid skin irritation. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to reassess progress and adjust the nicotine patch dosage. I will order a hemoglobin A1c to monitor his diabetes control. Continue allopurinol for gout. Encourage continued exercise and stress-reduction techniques, such as meditation or yoga. I will provide handouts on common smoking triggers.
